- Why this matters: This study provides strong evidence against the use of high-dose intravenous vitamin C in severe burn patients, as it does not improve outcomes and may increase mortality.

pubmed.title: Coagulopathy in obstetric bleeding: a narrative review and comparison with trauma-induced coagulopathy.
pubmed.bottom_line: This narrative review highlights that coagulopathy in postpartum hemorrhage has distinct pathophysiology—particularly acute obstetric coagulopathy involving pathological fibrinogen destruction—compared to trauma-induced coagulopathy, suggesting that current resuscitation guidelines based on trauma data may not optimally address the unique needs of obstetric patients. Clinicians should be aware of these differences to improve management and outcomes in obstetric bleeding.

pubmed.title: Clinical Prediction Models for Prognostication After Out-of-Hospital Cardiac Arrest: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis.
pubmed.bottom_line: This systematic review and meta-analysis of 39 cohorts (95,037 patients) found that the CAHP and OHCA scores have moderate sensitivity and specificity for predicting poor functional outcomes after out-of-hospital cardiac arrest, with the CAHP score supported by moderate certainty evidence; however, overall certainty is limited, highlighting the need for cautious clinical use and further validation.

pubmed.title: Dreaming during anaesthesia: a scoping review.
pubmed.bottom_line: This large scoping review finds that dreaming during anaesthesia is common, especially with ketamine and propofol, is usually pleasant, and is not a marker of intraoperative awareness; however, evidence is limited by inconsistent definitions and assessment methods, and more research is needed to clarify clinical significance and mechanisms.

pubmed.title: Gabapentin and Opioid Prescribing Trends among Adolescents Undergoing Common Procedures: A National Retrospective Cohort Study.
pubmed.bottom_line: This large retrospective cohort study found that while opioid-only prescriptions for adolescents after common surgeries declined from 2012 to 2022, gabapentin prescribing (alone or with opioids) increased, and adolescents who received both gabapentin and opioids were significantly more likely to obtain opioid refills, raising concerns about the effectiveness and safety of gabapentin as an opioid-sparing strategy in this population.

pubmed.title: Antidotes for Anticoagulation Reversal.
pubmed.journal: The New England journal of medicine
pubmed.bottom_line: This review highlights that while specific antidotes exist for some anticoagulants (e.g., idarucizumab for dabigatran, andexanet alfa for factor Xa inhibitors), evidence gaps and practical challenges—such as optimal dosing, monitoring, and safety—limit their use, underscoring the need for stronger clinical data and updated guidelines to improve anticoagulation reversal in bleeding emergencies.

pubmed.title: Sodium Bicarbonate for In-Hospital Cardiac Arrest: A Randomized Clinical Trial.
pubmed.bottom_line: In this large, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial, intravenous sodium bicarbonate administered during in-hospital cardiac arrest did not significantly improve rates of sustained return of spontaneous circulation or 30-day survival compared to placebo, indicating no clinical benefit for routine use in this setting.

pubmed.title: IVUS-Guided versus Angiography-Guided PCI in Unprotected Left Main Coronary Disease.
pubmed.journal: The New England journal of medicine
pubmed.bottom_line: In this randomized multicenter trial, IVUS-guided PCI did not improve clinical outcomes compared to angiography-guided PCI for unprotected left main coronary artery disease over a median 2.9-year follow-up, suggesting no added benefit of IVUS guidance in this setting.

pubmed.title: Subretinal Gene Therapy for X-Linked Retinoschisis.
pubmed.journal: The New England journal of medicine
pubmed.bottom_line: In this small phase 1/2 trial, subretinal AAV8-RS1 gene therapy for X-linked retinoschisis in children and adolescents was safe over 52 weeks, led to significant anatomical improvements and modest visual acuity gains, suggesting potential clinical benefit but requiring further study to confirm efficacy.

pubmed.title: Exa-cel in Children with Transfusion-Dependent β-Thalassemia or Sickle Cell Disease.
pubmed.journal: The New England journal of medicine
pubmed.bottom_line: In this phase 3, open-label study, exa-cel gene editing therapy resulted in transfusion independence in all evaluable children with transfusion-dependent β-thalassemia and elimination of vaso-occlusive crises in all evaluable children with sickle cell disease over a median 16–17 months of follow-up, though all experienced at least one serious adverse event; these findings suggest exa-cel may offer a transformative, though not

foamed.title: Update on total intravenous anesthesia in children
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This 2026 review summarizes recent advances in pediatric total intravenous anesthesia (TIVA), including improved pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic models, target-controlled infusion technology, EEG-guided dosing, and new agents like remimazolam, highlighting TIVA’s potential to reduce emergence delirium, postoperative nausea and vomiting, respiratory complications, and environmental impact compared to inhalational anesthesia.

foamed.title: Foreign body pulmonary embolisation
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This case report describes migration of a subdermal contraceptive implant (Implanon NXT) from the upper arm to the right pulmonary artery, presenting as loss of implant palpability and irregular bleeding, with diagnosis confirmed by imaging and successful endovascular retrieval; clinicians should consider migration in patients with non-palpable implants.

foamed.title: What does a good emergency physician look like?
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This article is an educational commentary reflecting on the diverse qualities that define an excellent emergency physician, emphasizing that outstanding clinicians may excel in different domains such as diagnosis, procedures, communication, or situational awareness, but does not present new research data or study results.

foamed.title: The Performance Art of Medicine
foamed.bottom_line: BOTTOM LINE: This blog post is an educational commentary discussing the concept of medicine as a performance art, emphasizing the value of incorporating performance skills into medical education to foster empathy, adaptability, and resilience in clinicians, but it does not present new clinical data or original research findings.

deep_dives.title: IVUS-Guided versus Angiography-Guided PCI in Unprotected Left Main Coronary Disease.
deep_dives.journal: The New England journal of medicine
deep_dives.bottom_line: In this randomized multicenter trial, IVUS-guided PCI did not improve clinical outcomes compared to angiography-guided PCI for unprotected left main coronary artery disease over a median 2.9-year follow-up, suggesting no added benefit of IVUS guidance in this setting.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: IVUS-guided PCI did not provide additional benefit over angiography-guided PCI in patients with unprotected left main coronary artery disease regarding major clinical outcomes at a median follow-up of 2.9 years.

- Study type: International, multicenter, open-label, randomized controlled trial
- Population/setting: 806 patients with unprotected left main coronary artery disease from multiple international centers
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: IVUS-guided PCI versus angiography-guided PCI
- Primary endpoints: Composite of any stroke, any myocardial infarction, any revascularization, or death from any cause at the longest follow-up
- Key results: Primary endpoint occurred in 33.7% of the IVUS group and 30.9% of the angiography group (hazard ratio, 1.11; 95% CI, 0.87 to 1.42; P = 0.40); incidence of death, myocardial infarction, or revascularization and safety events were similar between groups.
- Limitations:
- Open-label design may introduce bias
- Median follow-up of 2.9 years may not capture long-term differences
- Study powered for composite endpoint, not individual components
- Why this matters: This study suggests that routine use of IVUS guidance during PCI for unprotected left main coronary artery disease may not improve major clinical outcomes compared to standard angiographic guidance.

deep_dives.title: Sodium Bicarbonate for In-Hospital Cardiac Arrest: A Randomized Clinical Trial.
deep_dives.bottom_line: In this large, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial, intravenous sodium bicarbonate administered during in-hospital cardiac arrest did not significantly improve rates of sustained return of spontaneous circulation or 30-day survival compared to placebo, indicating no clinical benefit for routine use in this setting.
deep_dives.deep_dive_markdown:
BOTTOM LINE: Sodium bicarbonate did not significantly improve sustained return of spontaneous circulation compared to placebo in adults with in-hospital cardiac arrest.

- Study type: Randomized, parallel-group, double-blind, placebo-controlled clinical trial
- Population/setting: Adults with in-hospital cardiac arrest at 21 hospitals in Denmark who received at least one dose of epinephrine
- Intervention/exposure & comparator: Intravenous sodium bicarbonate (up to 100 mmol) versus placebo
- Primary endpoints: Sustained return of spontaneous circulation
- Key results: Sustained return of spontaneous circulation occurred in 39% of the sodium bicarbonate group and 37% of the placebo group (risk ratio 1.05, 95% CI 0.88-1.24; P = .62); 30-day survival was 12% vs 9.1% (risk ratio 1.25, 95% CI 0.84-1.88), and favorable neurologic outcome at 30 days was 8.1% vs 5.4% (risk ratio 1.39, 95% CI 0.82-2.34); alkalosis and hypernatremia were more common with sodium bicarbonate.
- Limitations:
- Single-country study may limit generalizability
- Only patients who received at least one dose of epinephrine were included
- Potential for unmeasured confounding factors
- Why this matters: This study provides evidence against the routine use of sodium bicarbonate during in-hospital cardiac arrest, informing clinical guidelines and practice.
